content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 20, 2016

Paula Deel
Vice President of the Board of Directors
Newberry Community Services District
P.O. Box 328
Newberry Springs, CA 92365

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-188

Dear Ms. Deel:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ and Section 1090. We are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

As required by statute, we have forwarded your request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Bernardino County District Attorney's Office. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) And the following advice is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor. (Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

Does the Act or Section 1090 prohibit you, as Vice President of the Board of Directors of the Newberry Community Services District, from taking part in a governmental decision on whether to pay an invoice for hardware or feed purchased by a District employee from Deel Plumbing, Heating & Air Conditioning ("Deel") given that your husband owns Deel?

CONCLUSION

Section 1090 does not prohibit you from taking part in a decision on whether to pay an invoice for urgently needed hardware or feed purchased from Deel.² The Act, however, would prohibit you from taking part in a decision on whether to pay an invoice for any product or service purchased from Deel because such a decision would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your interest in Deel as a business entity.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² However, we caution that neither you, nor Deel, nor any other agent of Deel should attempt to solicit additional business from the District or District employees.

File No. A-16-188
Page No. 2

FACTS

Newberry Springs is an unincorporated community located in the Mojave Desert at the foot
of the Newberry Mountains in San Bernardino County. The community's population was 2,637 as
of the 2010 census.

The Newberry Community Services District was formed in 1958 and provides community
residents with fire, park-and-recreation, and street-lighting services, among others. You are the Vice
President of the District's Board of Directors.

Your husband is the owner of Deel, a multi-purpose business located in Newberry Springs
that provides services and sells hardware and feed to local customers. You and your husband are the
only full time employees at Deels, and you are authorized to make management decisions on behalf
of the business.

The District has been a customer of Deel for over 36 years. District employees occasionally
purchase hardware or feed from Deel. There is no overarching contract between the District and
Deel governing these purchases. Deel does not provide the District any unique discount on these
purchases. And District employees are authorized to purchase hardware or feed on behalf of the
District. Deel subsequently submits invoices for these purchases to the District, and the District
reviews each invoice and pays it if appropriate. The next nearest hardware store is 22.7 miles away
in Barstow.

You have asked whether the Act or Section 1090 prohibits you from taking part in District
decisions relating to the payment of these invoices, and you provided additional facts by telephone.

ANALYSIS

Section 1090

Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities,
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their
agencies. (Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Under Section 1090, "the prohibited
act is the making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest." (People v. Honig
(1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void, regardless of
whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38
Cal.3d 633, 646-649.)

Although as the Vice President of the District's Board of Directors, you are subject to
Section 1090, your situation does not raise the type of contractual situation normally covered by
Section 1090. For example, Deel does not have, nor are you or your husband proposing to
negotiate, a contract with the District where the District agrees to purchase hardware exclusively
from Deel or Deel agrees to provide hardware at a discounted price. Instead, District employees
decide whether to purchase urgently needed hardware or feed for the District at Deel, Deel invoices

File No. A-16-188
Page No. 3

the District for the purchase, and the District's Board of Directors reviews the invoice and pays it if
appropriate. Although these individual retail transactions for urgently needed hardware or feed
between the District and Deel might be considered contractual in the most technical sense, they are
simply not the type of contracts that Section 1090 seeks to preclude. (See Hammond Advice Letter,
No. A-15-134.)

Even assuming we were to find such retail transactions to be prohibited under Section 1090,
which we do not, the "rule of necessity" exception would nonetheless apply to allow District
employees to continue to purchase urgently needed hardware or feed at Deel. In limited
circumstances, the rule of necessity has been applied to allow the making of a contract that Section
1090 would otherwise prohibit. (88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 110 (2005).) Under the rule of
necessity, a government agency may acquire essential goods or services despite the existence of a
conflict of interest, when no source other than that which triggers the conflict is available; the rule
"ensures that essential government functions are performed even where a conflict of interest exists."
(Eldridge v. Sierra View Hospital Dist. (1990) 224 Cal.App.3d 311, 321-322.)

Under your facts, if a District employee was unable to purchase urgently needed hardware
or feed on behalf of the District at Deel, he or she would have to travel 22.7 miles to the next
nearest hardware store in Barstow to purchase that hardware or feed. Having Deel as an option for
urgently needed hardware or feed allows the District to acquire essential goods while ensuring that
important government functions are performed without interruption.

Thus, Section 1090 neither prohibits you from taking part in a decision on whether to pay an
invoice for urgently needed hardware or feed purchased on behalf of the District from Deel, nor the
District from paying that invoice.

The Act

Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the interests of the public official or
a member of his or her immediate family. Section 87103 also identifies the financial interests that
may give rise to a conflict of interest under the Act, and of those interests, the facts presented and
the decisions at issue implicate the following:

• Any interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)).

• Any interest in a source of income to the official, including promised income, which aggregates
to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate family,
also known as the "personal financial effects" Rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 18702.5.)

File No. A-16-188
Page No. 4

Foreseeability and Materiality

Regulation 18701 provides that the financial effect of a governmental decision on an official's financial interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision. And Regulation 18702.1 provides that the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a decision on a business entity in which an official has a financial interest is material whenever the business entity offers to make a sale of a service or a product to the official's agency.

The effect of the District's decision on whether to pay an invoice for a product or service from Deel is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable because Deel would be the subject of that decision. And Deel offers to sell a product or service to the District when it agrees to provide the product or service and later submit an invoice for the product or service to the District. Therefore, a District decision on whether to pay an invoice for a product or service purchased from Deel would have a reasonably foreseeable material effect on your interest in Deel as a business entity.³ Accordingly, the Act would prohibit you from taking part in such a decision.⁴

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

_________________________

³ Because we have concluded that a disqualifying conflict of interest exists under the Act with respect to your financial interest in Deel as a business entity, we do not further analyze your other interests in Deel.

⁴ Assuming that you are a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 and that the decision that you are disqualified from under the Act is noticed at a public meeting, you must do all of the following with respect to that decision: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally identify each type of economic interest that is involved in the decision, as well as certain details of the economic interest, on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. (Section 87105; Regulation 18707.)

Section 1090

from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section I 090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall v. City of Taft ( I 962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Under Section 1090, "the prohibited 
act is the making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest." (People v. Honig 
( I 996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void, regardless of 
whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 
Cal.3d 633, 646-649.)

between the District and Deel might be considered contractual in the most technical sense, they are 
simply not the type of contracts that Section l 090 seeks to preclude. {See Hammond Advice Letter, 
No. A-15-134.)

which we do not, the "rule of necessity" exception would nonetheless apply to allow District

$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)). 
• 
Any interest in a source of income to the official, including promised income, which aggregates 
to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) 
• 
An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate family, 
also known as the "personal financial effects" Rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 18702.5.)

Regulation 1870 l provides that the financial effect of a governmental decision on an
